(4) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Understanding the Hexo Directory Structure
Now we'll get familiar with the Hexo directory structure. But heads up — in the previous post we only installed Hexo, we haven't actually created a Hexo project yet.
First we need to create a Hexo project. I'd suggest using
in your terminal to navigate to the path where you want the Hexo project to live, and then run the command. Otherwise, if your prompt shows
, your Hexo project will end up on your desktop:
(I usually name the folder after my blog). That's it — Hexo project created.
A freshly-created Hexo project is usually on the latest version. You can check the Hexo version directly in
Now let's open the Hexo project we just created. Personally I write blog posts in VSCode — I'll get into why later.
Once it's open you'll see the Hexo directory structure:
folder — just treat it as a cache for all the packages Hexo needs to run. Basically every package you install ends up in here.
Which is why people also call it the black hole.
In short, when you create a new post or page, Hexo uses one of these as the initial template. I'll get into these in more detail in a later post.
folder is where the original source files live — Markdown or HTML files all go in here. If you have a folder you don't want compiled, prefix it with
(underscore). Once a folder starts with an underscore, Hexo will skip it during compile and won't copy it to the
Worth noting: the articles we want compiled actually live under
, but the underscore in front of
doesn't mean Hexo skips it — that's a special folder. Personally I like organising my articles into folders inside
, so if you have a folder that contains drafts you don't want to publish yet, you can prefix that folder with an underscore and it won't get compiled into
is where Hexo themes live. By default the official theme
is included. I'll cover more themes later — for now, just know that Hexo will use the theme in this folder, combined with
, to compile and produce the static files.
is the main site configuration file for Hexo. Things like theme settings, blog name, and so on all get tweaked here. One thing worth being careful about: Hexo has its own
, and the theme also has its own
. These are two different files, so watch out.
For the rest of the series, when I need to reference Hexo's main config, I'll write
. Please keep this in mind so you don't mix the two up.
I'd recommend you start by updating the

# Site
title: Hexo # Blog name
subtitle: '' # Blog subtitle
description: '' # Blog description
keywords: # Blog keywords
author: John Doe # Blog author
language: en # Blog language — recommend changing this, though I'll leave it default for this post
timezone: '' # Standard timezone — set or skip based on preference. I personally don't set it.
# Blog language — recommend changing this, though I'll leave it default for this post
# Standard timezone — set or skip based on preference. I personally don't set it.
For reference, here's what mine looks like:

歡迎來到網頁世界 keywords: 前端,後端,全端,網頁,技術,網頁開發,前端開發,後端開發,JavaScript,HTML,CSS,SCSS author: Ray language: zh-TW timezone:

歡迎來到網頁世界
前端,後端,全端,網頁,技術,網頁開發,前端開發,後端開發,JavaScript,HTML,CSS,SCSS

is basically a list of files and folders to ignore. If you're not super familiar with git, the one thing to know is: anything you don't want in version control or pushed to GitHub gets listed here. Hexo's default ignores these:
In practice you don't actually edit
much — just good to know it's there.
is where the packages you install via npm get tracked and managed. Some commands are also written in here. For now, all you need to know is that any packages you install later end up recorded in

showed up around npm 5. Its main job is to lock down the exact version of every package installed at a given moment, so you always get the right versions on re-install. You won't manually edit this file — it only changes when you add, remove, or update packages, and even then it manages itself.
That's a wrap on today's Ironman post.
